strategies.name: Graham Defensive Investor Criteria
strategies.summary: Benjamin Graham's mechanical 7-test checklist for the passive ('defensive') investor: adequate size, strong financials, earnings stability, dividend record, growth, moderate P/E (<=15) and P/B (<=1.5, or P/E*P/B<=22.5).
strategies.rules: Score 1 point each: (1) size market_cap>=$2B; (2) current_ratio>=2.0 AND debt_to_equity<=1.0; (3) eps>0 (proxy for 10yr stability); (4) dividend>0; (5) revenue_growth>0 (proxy for 33% 10yr EPS growth); (6) price/eps<=15; (7) price/bvps<=1.5 OR P/E*P/B<=22.5. Buy when most pass; the valuation gate (tests 6-7) is binding.
strategies.parameters: PE_max=15; PB_max=1.5; PE_x_PB_max=22.5; current_ratio_min=2.0; debt_to_equity_max=1.0; require dividend>0,eps>0; size proxy market_cap>=2e9
strategies.computeSignal: PE=price/eps, PB=price/bvps. Count passed tests T/7. Valuation core V=clip(100*(22.5-PE*PB)/22.5,-100,100); if eps<=0 set V=-80. Quality multiplier Q=T/7. score=clip(V*(0.5+0.5*Q),-100,100). confidence=clip(0.4+0.5*(T/7),0,0.6); drop to 0.3 if eps<=0 or bvps<=0 (10yr history proxied).
strategies.computeProjection: Margin-of-safety (22.5-PE*PB) maps to a modest positive expected-return tilt; passing the strength tests (current_ratio>=2, low D/E) lowers the idiosyncratic-risk estimate.
sources: Benjamin Graham, 'The Intelligent Investor' (1973 rev.), Ch. 14
AAII Graham Defensive screens â€” https://www.aaii.com/stock-screens

strategies.name: Graham Number (Fair-Value Ceiling)
strategies.summary: Single fair-value formula from Graham's P/E<=15 and P/B<=1.5 limits: GN = sqrt(22.5 * EPS * BVPS), the maximum a defensive investor should pay for a profitable, asset-backed firm.
strategies.rules: GN=sqrt(22.5*eps*bvps), valid only when eps>0 and bvps>0. Buy when price<GN (discount); the larger the discount the stronger. Avoid when price>GN. Best for mature, asset-heavy, profitable firms; unreliable for asset-light/high-growth.
strategies.parameters: constant=22.5; require eps>0,bvps>0; strong-buy discount>=33%
strategies.computeSignal: If eps<=0 or bvps<=0 -> score 0, confidence 0.1. Else GN=sqrt(22.5*eps*bvps); D=(GN-price)/GN; score=clip(100*D,-100,100). confidence=0.55 base, up to 0.7 when eps and bvps both sizeable, down to 0.35 for asset-light sectors. Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: Discount-to-GN is a margin-of-safety proxy: feed magnitude into expected mean-reversion upside toward intrinsic value.
sources: Benjamin Graham, 'The Intelligent Investor' (1973) â€” 22.5 constant basis
GuruFocus Graham Number â€” https://www.gurufocus.com/term/graham-number

strategies.name: Graham Net-Net / NCAV (Deep Value)
strategies.summary: Graham's deep-value 'cigar-butt' screen (Security Analysis, 1934): buy a diversified basket trading below two-thirds of Net Current Asset Value (current assets minus total liabilities) â€” liquidation-value investing with built-in margin of safety.
strategies.rules: True NCAV = current assets - total_liabilities. With the snapshot, proxy NCAV_proxy = working_capital - max(0, total_liabilities - working_capital) approximated conservatively as working_capital (flag proxy). NCAV_per_share = NCAV_proxy/shares_out. Net-net buy when price < 0.67*NCAV_per_share. Hold a 20-30 name basket; sell on reaching NCAV or after 1-2 years.
strategies.parameters: threshold=0.67; basket 20-30; require NCAV_proxy>0; hold 1-2yr
strategies.computeSignal: NCAV_per_share=NCAV_proxy/shares_out. R=price/NCAV_per_share (if >0). score=clip(100*(1-R/0.67),-100,100): R<=0.67 strongly positive, R>1 capped negative. If NCAV_proxy<=0 -> score 0, conf 0.15. confidence=0.35 base (current-asset breakdown unavailable), up to 0.5 if working_capital and total_liabilities clean and positive. Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: Deep discount to liquidation value implies large mean-reversion upside but elevated distress risk; widen single-name risk and rely on diversification. Tilt scales with (0.67-R).
sources: Graham & Dodd, 'Security Analysis' (1934)
Oppenheimer (1986), 'Ben Graham's Net Current Asset Values', Financial Analysts Journal

strategies.name: Greenblatt Magic Formula
strategies.summary: Joel Greenblatt's combined ranking of cheapness (earnings yield = EBIT/EV) and quality (return on capital = EBIT/capital) from 'The Little Book That Beats the Market' (2005). Buys cheap-and-good businesses; reported 30.8% annualized 1988-2004.
strategies.rules: EV proxy = market_cap + net debt (net debt proxy = debt_to_equity*bvps*shares_out; if thin, EV=market_cap, flag proxy). EY=ebit/EV. ROC proxy = ebit/(working_capital + (total_assets-total_liabilities-working_capital)) i.e. ebit/net invested capital; fallback ebit/(total_assets-total_liabilities). Rank equities by EY and ROC; combined=rankEY+rankROC; buy lowest-combined tertile. Exclude financials/utilities; annual rebalance.
strategies.parameters: rank EY+ROC cross-sectional; buy best tertile; exclude financials/utilities; annual rebalance
strategies.computeSignal: Percentile-rank EY (pEY) and ROC (pROC) across equities. C=0.5*pEY+0.5*pROC. score=clip(200*(C-0.5),-100,100). If ebit<=0 force score<=-60. confidence=0.55, up to 0.7 when EV/ROC use real debt/asset fields, down to 0.4 when EV defaults to market_cap. Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: High EBIT/EV maps directly to a forward-return proxy; high ROC indicates quality compounding (lower-risk adjustment).
sources: Joel Greenblatt, 'The Little Book That Beats the Market' (2005, rev. 2010)
https://www.gurufocus.com/tutorial/article/57

strategies.name: Acquirer's Multiple (EV/EBIT)
strategies.summary: Tobias Carlisle's deep-value metric ('Deep Value', 2014): rank by the cheapest operating multiple EV/EBIT â€” Greenblatt's earnings yield without the ROC quality leg. Carlisle found the quality leg can hurt deep-value returns.
strategies.rules: AM=EV/ebit, EV=market_cap+net_debt (net debt proxy=debt_to_equity*bvps*shares_out; fallback EV=market_cap). Lower AM=cheaper. Rank ascending; buy cheapest quintile; require ebit>0; equal-weight; annual rebalance. Pure cheapness, no ROC filter.
strategies.parameters: EV=market_cap+net_debt; rank ascending EV/EBIT; buy cheapest quintile; min market_cap ~1B; require ebit>0; annual rebalance
strategies.computeSignal: EY=ebit/EV (inverse AM). Percentile-rank EY across equities; score=clip(200*(pEY-0.5),-100,100). Absolute fallback: AM<6 -> +60..+100, 6-10 mild +, 10-14 ~0, >14 negative. If ebit<=0 -> -70. confidence=0.6 with real net-debt, 0.45 when EV=market_cap. Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: EV/EBIT earnings yield is a clean forward-return proxy; strongest mean-reversion at lowest multiples. No quality offset â€” pair with a distress screen.
sources: Tobias E. Carlisle, 'The Acquirer's Multiple' (2014); 'Deep Value' (2014, Wiley)
Carlisle & Gray, 'Quantitative Value' (2012, Wiley)

strategies.name: Buffett Owner-Earnings Yield
strategies.summary: Warren Buffett's cash-earnings measure (1986 Berkshire letter): owner earnings = net income + non-cash charges - maintenance capex +/- working-capital change. Buy quality businesses when the owner-earnings yield is attractive vs price and the risk-free rate.
strategies.rules: OE proxy = fcf_per_share (FCF nets capex; standard owner-earnings proxy). OEY=fcf_per_share/price. Attractive when OEY > risk_free + ~3-4% equity premium. Require fcf_per_share>0.
strategies.parameters: OE proxy=fcf_per_share; OEY=fcf_per_share/price; attractive when OEY>rf+0.03; require fcf>0
strategies.computeSignal: OEY=fcf_per_share/price. X=OEY-risk_free_rate. score=clip(100*(X/0.06),-100,100) (6% excess -> +100). If fcf_per_share<=0 -> -50. confidence=0.5 (FCF proxies OE without maintenance-vs-growth capex split), up to 0.6 if FCF clearly positive/stable. Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: OEY is a direct expected-cash-return proxy; use as baseline expected return and add growth from revenue_growth. Higher, stable OEY implies a lower required risk premium.
sources: Warren Buffett, Berkshire Hathaway 1986 Shareholder Letter, 'Owner Earnings' appendix
Robert Hagstrom, 'The Warren Buffett Way' (1994)

strategies.name: Buffett Quality-at-Fair-Price (ROE/Moat)
strategies.summary: Buffett's evolution from Graham: buy durable-moat businesses (high stable ROE>=15%, high margins, low debt) at a fair price. Codified in Validea/Hagstrom quantitative interpretations.
strategies.rules: Quality gates: ROE=eps/bvps>=15%; roa>=7%; net_margin>=10%; debt_to_equity<=0.5; revenue_growth>0; fcf_per_share>0. Fair-price gate: P/E reasonable for the quality. Buy when quality passes AND price is fair.
strategies.parameters: ROE_min=0.15; ROA_min=0.07; net_margin_min=0.10; debt_to_equity_max=0.5; revenue_growth>0; fcf>0
strategies.computeSignal: Q in [0,1] = mean of q_roe=clip((eps/bvps)/0.20,0,1), q_roa=clip(roa/0.12,0,1), q_margin=clip(net_margin/0.20,0,1), q_debt=clip((0.5-debt_to_equity)/0.5,0,1), q_growth=clip(revenue_growth/0.10,0,1). price_score=clip((25-PE)/25,-1,1) (eps<=0 -> -1). score=clip(100*Q*(0.5+0.5*price_score),-100,100). confidence=clip(0.45+0.2*Q,0,0.6) (single snapshot can't verify 10yr ROE durability). Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: Project expected return ~ ROE*retention + dividend yield (sustainable-growth model); moat lowers downside-risk estimate, fair-price gate prevents overpaying.
sources: Robert Hagstrom, 'The Warren Buffett Way' (1994/2013)
Mary Buffett & David Clark, 'Buffettology' (1997)
https://blog.validea.com/building-a-quantitative-strategy-based-on-warren-buffetts-approach/

strategies.name: Novy-Marx Gross Profitability
strategies.summary: Robert Novy-Marx's gross-profitability premium (JFE 2013): gross profits scaled by total assets predict the cross-section of returns about as well as book-to-market; profitable firms earn higher returns.
strategies.rules: GP=(sales-COGS)/total_assets. COGS not provided: proxy GP=ebit/total_assets (operating-profitability flavor), or use gross_margin if present. Rank GP across equities; long top tercile, avoid bottom. Slow-moving annual ranking.
strategies.parameters: metric=gross_profits/total_assets; cross-sectional tercile; long top 33%; rebalance annually
strategies.computeSignal: Compute GP (true if COGS/gross_margin available, else ebit/total_assets). Percentile-rank across equities. score=clip((GP_pct-50)*2,-100,100). Optional level blend: GP>0.33 +10, GP<0.10 -10. confidence=0.75 when true gross profit computable, 0.55 on ebit proxy. Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: Profitability premium ~3.8%/yr long-short; a top-tercile name gets a positive tilt up to ~+3-4%/yr vs universe mean, decaying slowly. Low turnover; risk near market beta.
sources: Robert Novy-Marx, 'The Other Side of Value: The Gross Profitability Premium', JFE 108(1), 2013 â€” https://rnm.simon.rochester.edu/research/OSoV.pdf

strategies.name: Quality Minus Junk (QMJ)
strategies.summary: Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen's QMJ (AQR; Review of Accounting Studies 2019): quality = profitable + growing + safe + (payout). Long high-quality, short junk; quality earns high risk-adjusted returns.
strategies.rules: Composite z-score from four equal-weight pillars ranked cross-sectionally: Profitability (gross profit/assets, net_margin, roa, fcf/price); Growth (revenue_growth, proxy for 5yr profit growth); Safety (low debt_to_equity, low beta, high current_ratio, low return vol); Payout (dividend>0 and dividend_growth>=0, retained_earnings>0). Long top 30%, short bottom 30%.
strategies.parameters: pillars profitability/growth/safety/payout equal weight; long top 30%, short bottom 30%; safety uses beta+debt_to_equity+current_ratio
strategies.computeSignal: Per equity, z=(x-cross_mean)/cross_std for each metric. Profitability_z=mean(z(net_margin),z(roa),z(gp/assets),z(fcf/price)). Growth_z=z(revenue_growth). Safety_z=mean(z(-debt_to_equity),z(-beta),z(current_ratio),z(-vol)). Payout_z=z(dividend>0?dividend_growth:0)+bonus if retained_earnings>0. Quality=mean of 4. score=clip(Quality_z*33,-100,100). confidence=0.7 base, -0.15 (growth uses single-period revenue_growth), +0.1 if all pillars valid. Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: High-quality names get a modest positive tilt and notably lower drawdown/variance (safety pillar); use lower beta to reduce projected risk.
sources: Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen, 'Quality Minus Junk', Review of Accounting Studies 24, 2019 â€” https://www.aqr.com/Insights/Research/Working-Paper/Quality-Minus-Junk

strategies.name: High Return-on-Capital Compounder
strategies.summary: Buffett/Greenblatt-style quality compounding: persistently high return on capital (ROA/ROE/ROIC) with strong FCF and low leverage signals a durable business that compounds shareholder value â€” the standalone 'quality' leg, ranked across the universe.
strategies.rules: ROE=eps/bvps; ROC=ebit/(total_assets-total_liabilities); ROA given. Bullish when ROA>=8-10%, ROE>=15%, growing fcf_per_share>0, debt_to_equity<1.0. Rank return-on-capital across equities; favor top quintile.
strategies.parameters: roa_min~0.08-0.10; roe_min~0.15; ROC=ebit/(total_assets-total_liabilities); debt_to_equity_max~1.0; rank top quintile
strategies.computeSignal: ROE=eps/bvps; ROC=ebit/max(total_assets-total_liabilities,eps); rank ROC_pct and ROA_pct across equities. quality_rank=0.6*ROC_pct+0.4*ROA_pct. score=clip((quality_rank-50)*2,-100,100). +10 if ROE>=15% and fcf>0; -15 if debt_to_equity>2; -20 if roa<0 or eps<0. confidence=0.7 base, +0.1 if ROE and FCF strong, -0.1 (ROE uses book-value proxy). Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: High incremental ROIC supports above-average reinvestment-driven growth: positive expected-return tilt, lower fundamental volatility, faster mean-reversion in ROC than passive.
sources: Joel Greenblatt, 'The Little Book That Beats the Market' (2005)
Robert G. Hagstrom, 'The Warren Buffett Way' (1994)

strategies.name: Fama-French 5-Factor (RMW + CMA)
strategies.summary: Fama & French's five-factor model (JFE 2015) adds profitability (RMW, Robust Minus Weak) and investment (CMA, Conservative Minus Aggressive) to the 3-factor model. Robust-profitability and conservative-investment firms outperform.
strategies.rules: RMW: operating profitability OP=ebit/book_equity (book_equity=bvps*shares_out; fallback ebit/total_assets); robust=top 30%. CMA: investment=asset growth (no prior-year assets; proxy aggressiveness via high revenue_growth + negative FCF + no dividend); conservative=low. Long robust+conservative, avoid weak+aggressive. Corroborate with the supplied daily SMB/HML factor loadings.
strategies.parameters: RMW=ebit/book_equity top/bottom 30%; CMA aggressiveness proxy=z(revenue_growth)+z(-fcf)+z(no dividend); book_equity=bvps*shares_out
strategies.computeSignal: OP=ebit/book_equity. rmw_pct=percentile(OP) across equities; rmw_score=(rmw_pct-50)*2. Aggressiveness A=z(revenue_growth)+z(-fcf_per_share)+z(dividend==0?1:0); cma_score=clip(-A*33,-100,100). score=clip(0.6*rmw_score+0.4*cma_score,-100,100). If ebit<=0 force <=-30. confidence=0.55 (CMA leg uses asset-growth proxy; RMW clean). Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: RMW ~+0.25%/mo, CMA positive-but-modest premium; tilt expected return up for robust+conservative names and use factor loadings (mkt/SMB/HML) for the systematic component and variance.
sources: Fama & French, 'A Five-Factor Asset Pricing Model', JFE 116(1), 2015 â€” https://tevgeniou.github.io/EquityRiskFactors/bibliography/FiveFactor.pdf
Kenneth R. French Data Library (RMW/CMA definitions)

strategies.name: Peter Lynch PEG / GARP
strategies.summary: Peter Lynch's Growth-At-a-Reasonable-Price ('One Up on Wall Street'): buy companies whose earnings growth exceeds the P/E paid (PEG<1), with balance-sheet health filters.
strategies.rules: PEG=(P/E)/(growth% as whole number). P/E=price/eps; growth proxy=revenue_growth*100 (no EPS-growth history). PEG~1 fair, <1 attractive, >2 expensive. Bullish when PEG<1 and growth 15-30% (avoid >50% as unsustainable). Filters: debt_to_equity<0.6 (pref 0.25), current_ratio>=1.0, eps>0.
strategies.parameters: peg_buy<1.0,peg_fair=1.0,peg_sell>2.0; growth_floor=15%,sweet 20-25%,ceiling=50%; debt_to_equity_max=0.6; current_ratio_min=1.0
strategies.computeSignal: If eps<=0 or revenue_growth<=0 -> score 0, conf 0.2. PE=price/eps; g=revenue_growth*100; PEG=PE/g. score=clamp(100*(1-PEG),-100,100). If g<15 or g>50 scale score*0.5. Quality: debt_to_equity>0.6 -> -20; current_ratio<1 -> -15; debt_to_equity<0.25 and current_ratio>=1.5 -> +10. clamp [-100,100]. confidence=0.7 base, +0.15 if filters pass, -0.3 (revenue_growth proxies EPS growth); range 0.3-0.85.
strategies.computeProjection: Sub-1.0 PEG implies underpriced growth; project annual excess return proportional to (1-PEG) capped ~+6%/yr, fading as PEG reverts to 1.0. Use the asset beta for variance.
sources: Peter Lynch, 'One Up on Wall Street' (1989); 'Beating the Street' (1993)
ChartMill Peter Lynch screener documentation

strategies.name: O'Neil CAN SLIM
strategies.summary: William O'Neil's CAN SLIM growth system ('How to Make Money in Stocks'): blends strong earnings growth with technical leadership (relative strength, new highs) and overall market direction.
strategies.rules: C/A=earnings strength: eps>0 and revenue_growth>=25% (proxy). N=new highs: price within 15% of 252-day high. L=leader: RS rank>=80 in the universe by 126-252d return. S=supply/demand: rising price, smaller shares_out. I=institutional: proxy via persistent up-trend. M=market: only long when SPY>200d SMA. Buy near breakouts while M positive.
strategies.parameters: RS_rank_min=80; new_high prox within 15% of 252d high; earnings_growth_min=25% (revenue_growth proxy); market filter SPY>200d SMA; RS lookback 126-252d
strategies.computeSignal: RS=trailing 126d return; rank to percentile 0-100. high252=max close last 252d; prox=close/high252. marketOK=SPY>200d SMA. earningsOK=eps>0 and revenue_growth>=0.25. base=(RS_pct-50)*2. +20 if prox>=0.85, +15 if prox>=0.99; +15 if earningsOK, -15 if eps<=0. If NOT marketOK multiply positive score by 0.3. clamp [-100,100]. confidence=0.6 base, +0.2 if earningsOK and marketOK, -0.2 (quarterly EPS acceleration/sponsorship proxied); range 0.3-0.85.
strategies.computeProjection: Top-RS names near new highs continue to outperform 3-12 months; project positive drift scaled by RS percentile, cut sharply when M negative. Higher idiosyncratic risk â€” widen variance.
sources: William J. O'Neil, 'How to Make Money in Stocks' (4th ed., 2009)
AAII CAN SLIM screen â€” https://www.aaii.com/stocks/screens/78

strategies.name: Chowder Rule (Dividend Growth)
strategies.summary: Dividend-growth screen (Seeking Alpha 'Chowder' / David Fish CCC lists): Chowder Number = current yield + 5yr dividend growth rate, approximating potential total return with a margin of safety against sector-specific thresholds.
strategies.rules: Chowder = yield% + dgr%. Thresholds: non-utility yield>=3% needs Chowder>=12; non-utility yield<3% needs >=15; utility-like (slow-growth high-yield) needs >=8. Pass when above threshold AND payout<~75% of EPS. Non-payer neutral.
strategies.parameters: dgr window proxy=single dividend_growth field; T={12 if yield>=3 else 15; 8 for utility-like}; safe payout 0.3-0.6; cut at payout>0.9; squash scale=6
strategies.computeSignal: yield_pct=100*dividend/price; dgr_pct=100*dividend_growth (proxy for 5yr DGR). chowder=yield_pct+dgr_pct. Pick T (15 if yield<3 else 12; 8 if utility-like with yield>=4 and dgr<=6). margin=chowder-T. score=clamp(squash(margin,scale=6),-100,100). If dividend<=0 -> score 0, conf 0.15. If dividend/eps>0.9 multiply score by 0.5. confidence=clamp(0.4+0.4*min(1,|margin|/6),0,1), cap 0.7 when payout in 0.3-0.6 band.
strategies.computeProjection: Chowder Number itself estimates expected annual total return; feed expected_annual_return~chowder/100 capped 0.20 as a mild upward drift for 1Y+ horizons; not a short-horizon vol input.
sources: Sure Dividend, 'The Chowder Rule Explained' â€” https://www.suredividend.com/the-chowder-rule-explained/
David Fish CCC Lists, DripInvesting.org

strategies.name: Dogs of the Dow (High-Yield Blue Chips)
strategies.summary: Michael O'Higgins ('Beating the Dow', 1991): each January buy the 10 highest-yielding Dow stocks, equal-weight, hold one year. An elevated yield on a quality blue chip signals a temporarily depressed price (value/mean-reversion).
strategies.rules: Rank dividend-paying equities by trailing yield (dividend/price). Top 10 = buy, equal-weight; rest hold/avoid. Hold 12 months, re-rank. Entering top decile is bullish; falling out loses the signal. Exclude non-payers.
strategies.parameters: rank set=dividend-paying equities (Dow proxy); top 10 by yield equal-weight; annual rebalance; quality dampener at payout>1.0; squash scale=0.35
strategies.computeSignal: yield=dividend/price. Compute cross-sectional yield percentile among payers. score=squash(percentile-0.5,scale=0.35) (top ~90th pct ~+76). Non-payers -> score 0, conf 0.1. confidence=clamp(0.35+0.4*|percentile-0.5|*2,0,1). Dampen score*0.6 if dividend/eps>1.0 (yield high because earnings collapsed â€” likely cut).
strategies.computeProjection: Mild value/mean-reversion tilt: high relative yield implies depressed price likely to revert. Add ~+2% annualized drift for top-decile Dogs; neutral on vol.
sources: Michael O'Higgins & John Downes, 'Beating the Dow' (1991)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dogs_of_the_Dow

strategies.name: Small Dogs of the Dow (Low-Priced 5)
strategies.summary: O'Higgins concentrated variant: from the 10 Dogs take the 5 lowest-priced, equal-weight. Lower-priced shares historically show larger percentage moves, amplifying the rebound at the cost of diversification.
strategies.rules: From the top-10 yielders, rank by nominal price ascending and select the 5 lowest-priced. Equal-weight, hold one year, rebalance. High-yield AND low-priced is strongest; high-yield high-priced weaker; not a Dog neutral.
strategies.parameters: stage-1=top 10 yielders; stage-2=5 lowest-priced; equal weight; annual rebalance; confidence cap 0.5; squash scale=0.3
strategies.computeSignal: Stage 1 yield percentile (as Dogs). Stage 2 among top-10 yielders compute inverse-price rank. For Dogs: combined=0.5*(yield_pct-0.5)+0.5*((1-price_pct)-0.5); for non-Dogs combined=0.4*(yield_pct-0.5). score=squash(combined,scale=0.3). confidence=clamp(0.3+0.4*min(1,|combined|/0.5),0,1), cap 0.5 (low diversification). Non-payers -> 0.
strategies.computeProjection: Same value/mean-reversion tilt as Dogs but stronger and noisier; apply +2% drift to the 5 selected and widen projected vol x1.1 for concentration.
sources: Michael O'Higgins & John Downes, 'Beating the Dow' (1991, rev.)
AAII 'Dogs of the Dow: Low Priced 5' screen â€” https://www.aaii.com/stocks/screens/83

strategies.name: Shareholder Yield (Mebane Faber)
strategies.summary: Meb Faber ('Shareholder Yield', 2013): total cash returned = dividend yield + net buyback yield + net debt-paydown yield. Outperforms dividend yield alone because buybacks and deleveraging are tax-advantaged and harder to fake.
strategies.rules: Shareholder Yield = div yield + net buyback yield + net debt-paydown yield. No buyback/debt-flow series: proxy. divYield=dividend/price; buyback proxy=max(0, fcf_per_share-dividend)/price (covered FCF surplus funds repurchases); debt tilt=clamp(1-debt_to_equity,-0.5,0.5)*0.03. Rank universe; overweight top quintile.
strategies.parameters: divYield=dividend/price; buyback proxy=max(0,fcf-dividend)/price; debt tilt=clamp(1-debt_to_equity,-0.5,0.5)*0.03; ref yield=4%; squash scale=0.05; conf cap 0.55
strategies.computeSignal: shareholderYieldProxy=divYield+max(0,(fcf_per_share-dividend))/price+clamp(1-debt_to_equity,-0.5,0.5)*0.03. score=squash(shareholderYieldProxy-0.04,scale=0.05) (~9% -> +76). confidence=clamp(0.35+0.4*min(1,proxy/0.10),0,1) cap 0.55 (buyback/debt proxied). Crypto/ETF with fcf=0 -> score 0, conf 0.1.
strategies.computeProjection: High total shareholder yield is a value/quality signal with positive forward excess returns; use proxy capped 0.12 as a small additive 1Y drift premium; neutral on vol.
sources: Mebane T. Faber, 'Shareholder Yield: A Better Approach to Dividend Investing' (2013)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shareholder_yield

strategies.name: Dividend Safety (Yield + Payout/Coverage)
strategies.summary: Canonical income safety screen (CFA Institute 'Analysis of Dividend Safety'; Simply Safe Dividends): combine an attractive yield with a sustainable payout. Dividends covered by a low fraction of earnings and FCF, with manageable leverage, are far less likely to be cut.
strategies.rules: EPS payout=dividend/eps (safe 0.30-0.60, caution 0.60-0.75, danger >0.75). FCF payout=dividend/fcf_per_share (sustainable <1.0). Leverage check debt_to_equity below norm, current_ratio>=1. Buy yield>=2.5% with safe payout, FCF coverage>1x, healthy balance sheet. Flag/avoid payout>1 or FCF payout>1.
strategies.parameters: safe eps_payout 0.3-0.6 (caution 0.75); FCF coverage>=1.0x; leverage flag debt_to_equity>1.5; liquidity flag current_ratio<1; yield neutral 2%, attractive 6%; safety weight 0.6 vs yield 0.4
strategies.computeSignal: yield=dividend/price; eps_payout=dividend/eps; fcf_payout=dividend/fcf_per_share. safetyScore start 1.0: if eps_payout>0.6 subtract (eps_payout-0.6)/0.4 (cap 0.5); if fcf_payout>1 subtract min(0.4,fcf_payout-1); if debt_to_equity>1.5 subtract 0.15; if current_ratio<1 subtract 0.1; clamp [0,1]. yieldAttr=clamp((yield-0.02)/0.04,-1,1). raw=(2*safetyScore-1)*0.6+yieldAttr*0.4. score=clamp(100*raw,-100,100). dividend<=0 -> 0, conf 0.1. If eps<=0 or fcf<=0 cap |score| at 30. confidence=clamp(0.4+0.4*safetyScore,0,0.85).
strategies.computeProjection: Quality/low-risk income signal: well-covered dividends correlate with lower drawdown; narrow projected downside vol x0.95 for high-safety names; no upside-drift claim.
sources: CFA Institute, 'Analysis of Dividend Safety' â€” https://analystprep.com/study-notes/cfa-level-2/analysis-of-dividend-safety/
Simply Safe Dividends â€” Dividend Safety Score methodology

strategies.name: Dividend Growth / Aristocrats (Yield-on-Cost)
strategies.summary: The dividend-growth discipline behind the S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats (25+ consecutive annual increases) and Fish's CCC lists: own firms that reliably raise dividends, letting yield-on-cost compound; durable growing payouts signal managerial discipline and earnings durability.
strategies.rules: Require positive durable dividend growth. Favor dividend_growth in a healthy 5-12% range with payout<~65% (room to keep raising). Estimated 10yr yield-on-cost = current_yield*(1+g)^10. Buy when growth strong and sustainable; downgrade when growth stalls or payout stretched. Non-payers excluded.
strategies.parameters: growth proxy=dividend_growth; healthy band 5-12%; payout cap 0.65; YoC horizon 10yr; growthScore neutral g=4%, scale 0.05; weights growth 0.6/YoC 0.4; conf cap 0.7
strategies.computeSignal: yield0=dividend/price; g=dividend_growth; yoc10=yield0*(1+g)^10. growthScore=squash(g-0.04,scale=0.05). yocBoost=squash(yoc10-2*yield0,scale=0.03). payout=dividend/eps; if payout>0.65 multiply growthScore by max(0.3,1-(payout-0.65)/0.35). score=clamp(0.6*growthScore+0.4*yocBoost,-100,100). If dividend<=0 or g<=0 -> at most slightly negative, conf 0.2. confidence=clamp(0.4+0.4*min(1,|g|/0.10),0,1), cap 0.7 (streak unverified).
strategies.computeProjection: Compounding-income + quality signal: project forward total-return drift = yield0+g (Gordon-style) capped 0.15 annualized for 1Y+ horizons on sustainable names; neutral on short-horizon vol.
sources: S&P Dow Jones Indices, 'S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats' methodology
David Fish, 'U.S. Dividend Champions' (CCC), DripInvesting.org
Gordon (1959) growth model

strategies.name: Cross-Sectional Momentum (12-1, Jegadeesh-Titman)
strategies.summary: The relative-strength momentum anomaly: rank assets by trailing 12-month return skipping the most recent month, long winners short losers. Jegadeesh & Titman (1993); basis of the academic UMD/MOM factor. (Distinct from app's single-asset 'momentum': this is the cross-sectional ranking with the skip-month spec.)
strategies.rules: Formation: cumulative return from t-252 to t-21 (12m excluding last ~21d to avoid 1-month reversal). Cross-sectionally rank the 24 assets monthly; equal-weight long top tertile, short bottom tertile (or top/bottom ~8). Hold 1 month, re-rank.
strategies.parameters: formation_lookback=252; skip_recent=21; holding=21; long=top 33%, short=bottom 33%; equal-weighted
strategies.computeSignal: r_12_1=Close[t-21]/Close[t-252]-1 per asset. z=(r_12_1-mean)/std across the 24 assets. score=clip(z*40,-100,100) (+2.5 sigma ~+100). confidence=min(1,n_valid/24)*min(1,history_days/252); reduce when <252d or cross-sectional std near zero.
strategies.computeProjection: Winners' next-month expected excess return is positive (~0.5-1%/mo spread); map score to an expected-return overlay proportional to score; note negative skew / crash risk â€” raise downside variance after extreme market drawdowns.
sources: Jegadeesh & Titman (1993), 'Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers', Journal of Finance 48(1):65-91
https://alphaarchitect.com/momentum-factor-investing-30-years-of-out-of-sample-data/

strategies.name: Time-Series (Absolute) Momentum with Vol Scaling
strategies.summary: Trade each asset on the sign of its own trailing ~12-month excess return (long if positive, short/flat if negative), sizing inversely to ex-ante volatility to target constant risk. Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012) across 58 futures. (Merges the two TSMOM/absolute-momentum research items.)
strategies.rules: signal=sign(trailing 12m excess return = 12m total return - 12m risk-free). Long if >0, short/flat if <0. weight=sign*(target_vol/sigma_ex_ante), target_vol~40% annualized (or 10-15% unlevered). sigma=EWMA of daily squared returns (delta=0.94 RiskMetrics) annualized by sqrt(252). Rebalance monthly.
strategies.parameters: lookback=252; target_vol=0.40 (or 0.10-0.15 unlevered); vol estimator EWMA delta=0.94, annualize sqrt(252); long/short or long/cash
strategies.computeSignal: r_12m=Close[t]/Close[t-252]-1; rf_12m=trailing-12m sum of daily risk-free. excess=r_12m-rf_12m. sigma_annual=sqrt(252*EWMA(r_daily^2,0.94)). raw=excess/sigma_annual. score=clip(tanh(raw)*100,-100,100). confidence=min(1,history_days/252)*clip(|excess|/sigma_annual,0.3,1).
strategies.computeProjection: Positive trend predicts continued positive excess return ~1yr; feed sign*magnitude into expected return; use sigma_ex_ante as forward vol and scale to target_vol. 'Crisis alpha' lowers projected tail risk in trending bear markets.
sources: Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012), 'Time Series Momentum', JFE 104(2):228-250
https://www.aqr.com/Insights/Research/Journal-Article/Time-Series-Momentum

strategies.name: Dual Momentum / GEM (Antonacci)
strategies.summary: Combines relative momentum (stronger of US vs ex-US equities) with absolute momentum (hold equities only if they beat T-bills, else bonds). Gary Antonacci ('Dual Momentum Investing', 2014); GEM matched/beat the S&P 500 with ~half the max drawdown.
strategies.rules: Monthly on trailing 12m total returns: (1) relative â€” pick higher-return equity proxy (SPY vs ex-US/QQQ proxy); (2) absolute â€” if winner's 12m > 12m T-bill, hold the winning equity; else hold bonds/GLD (defensive). With this universe SPY/QQQ are US-equity proxies, GLD/cash the defensive sleeve (flag proxy if no true bond ETF).
strategies.parameters: lookback=252 (12m total return); rebalance monthly; assets: US equity (SPY), competitor (ex-US or QQQ proxy), defensive (bond ETF or GLD); absolute hurdle = trailing 12m risk-free
strategies.computeSignal: Per candidate r_12m from closes, rf_12m from factor series. abs_mom=r_12m-rf_12m. rel_rank=r_12m minus best competitor's. Equity score=clip(50*sign(abs_mom)+50*sign(rel_winner),-100,100). Defensive (bond/GLD) score positive when equities fail the absolute filter, else low. confidence=min(1,history_days/252), higher when |abs_mom| far from hurdle.
strategies.computeProjection: Regime/allocation signal: positive equity absolute momentum -> equity-like return and vol; negative -> defensive asset's lower return and much lower drawdown. Sets risk-on vs risk-off portfolio beta.
sources: Antonacci (2014), 'Dual Momentum Investing', McGraw-Hill
Antonacci (2013), 'Risk Premia Harvesting Through Dual Momentum', SSRN 2042750

strategies.name: Absolute Momentum Trend Overlay (Antonacci)
strategies.summary: Single-asset trend filter: be invested only when an asset's trailing 12-month return exceeds the risk-free rate, else go to cash/defensive. Antonacci (2013) â€” mainly cuts volatility and drawdown; a universal trend-following overlay.
strategies.rules: Monthly compute trailing 12m excess return = 12m total return - 12m cash return. If >0 stay long/risk-on; if <=0 exit to cash/defensive. Binary per-asset switch, no peer comparison. Optional: require 2 consecutive confirmations to cut whipsaw.
strategies.parameters: lookback=252; hurdle=trailing 12m risk-free; rebalance monthly; optional 2-reading confirmation
strategies.computeSignal: r_12m=Close[t]/Close[t-252]-1; rf_12m=trailing-12m sum of daily risk-free. excess=r_12m-rf_12m. sigma=sqrt(252*var of daily returns). score=clip(100*tanh((excess/sigma)*1.5),-100,100). confidence=min(1,history_days/252), rising with |excess|/sigma.
strategies.computeProjection: Acts as a gate/regime multiplier: multiply other bullish expected returns by max(0,score/100) so positions are only taken in confirmed uptrends. Lowers projected max drawdown and downside vol vs buy-and-hold.
sources: Antonacci (2013), 'Absolute Momentum: A Simple Rule-Based Strategy and Universal Trend-Following Overlay', SSRN 2244633

strategies.name: 52-Week High Proximity / Breakout (George-Hwang)
strategies.summary: Rank assets by how close price is to its 52-week high; nearness predicts higher future returns and subsumes much of Jegadeesh-Titman momentum (anchoring). George & Hwang (2004). Also serves as a Donchian/Darvas long-term breakout entry. (Merges the two 52-week-high research items.)
strategies.rules: PR=Close/HH252 (HH252=highest high over 252 days), in (0,1]; 1.0 = at a new 52-week high. Rank cross-sectionally; long top 30% (nearest high), avoid bottom 30%. Breakout variant: enter when close breaks prior-day HH252; trail exit on a 50-day low or PR<0.75. George-Hwang returns do NOT reverse long-run.
strategies.parameters: high_window=252; long=top 30%, avoid=bottom 30%; entry breakout=close>prior HH252; exit=50-day low or PR<0.75; rebalance monthly
strategies.computeSignal: high252=max(High[t-252..t]); PR=Close/high252. score=clip((PR-0.85)/0.15*100,-100,100) (PR=1.0 ~+100, 0.85 ~0, <=0.70 ~-100); flip strongly negative on a new 52-week low. confidence=min(1,history_days/252), reduced if highly volatile/noisy.
strategies.computeProjection: Near-high names carry a positive tilt that persists without long-run reversal (George-Hwang); weight expected return up for high-PR names. A failed breakout gives a clean stop at the high-water mark, bounding projected downside.
sources: George & Hwang (2004), 'The 52-Week High and Momentum Investing', Journal of Finance 59(5):2145-2176
Darvas (1960), 'How I Made $2,000,000 in the Stock Market'

strategies.name: Relative-Strength Rotation (Faber)
strategies.summary: Rank a universe of ETFs/sectors by recent relative strength and hold the top-N each month, gated by a long-term trend filter on the broad market. Faber & Richardson, 'Relative Strength Strategies for Investing' (Cambria); beat buy-and-hold ~70% of the time.
strategies.rules: Composite RS = average of 1/3/6/12-month total returns. Hold top N (e.g. top 3 equal-weight). Rebalance monthly; replace holdings that fall out. Trend gate: only hold equity sleeves while SPY>200d SMA, else cash.
strategies.parameters: ranking lookbacks {21,63,126,252} averaged; top_N=3 equal-weight; rebalance monthly; trend filter SPY>SMA200; cash when below
strategies.computeSignal: rs=mean(Close[t]/Close[t-21]-1, .../63, .../126, .../252). z=(rs-mean)/std across rotation universe. base=clip(z*40,-100,100). If SPY<200d SMA multiply by 0.0-0.3 (risk-off). confidence=min(1,history_days/252)*(1 if trend filter agrees with sign else 0.5).
strategies.computeProjection: Allocation signal: capital into top-N highest-RS assets, zero into the rest; the SMA gate caps drawdown by exiting to cash in bear regimes (the main vol/drawdown lever).
sources: Faber & Richardson (2010), 'Relative Strength Strategies for Investing', Cambria
Faber (2007), 'A Quantitative Approach to Tactical Asset Allocation'

strategies.name: Risk-Adjusted (Frog-in-the-Pan) Momentum
strategies.summary: Refines 12-1 momentum by preferring smooth, high-consistency trends over jumpy ones. Da, Gurun & Warachka (2014): continuous information (steady gains, high % up-days) produces stronger, more persistent momentum.
strategies.rules: Compute 12-1 momentum (t-252 to t-21). Information-discreteness ID=sign(PRET)*(%down_days-%up_days). Low ID=smooth (stronger), high ID=jumpy (weaker). Long high-momentum AND low-ID (smooth winners). Equivalently scale momentum by information ratio (return/vol). Rebalance monthly.
strategies.parameters: formation 252 to 21; smoothness window=formation; ID=sign(PRET)*(pct_down-pct_up); prefer low ID; or IR=formation_return/formation_daily_vol
strategies.computeSignal: z_mom=cross-sectional z of r_12_1. IR=r_12_1/(daily_std*sqrt(252)); consistency C=(up_days-down_days)/total signed by trend. smoothness multiplier m=clip(0.5+0.5*normalized(IR or -ID),0.3,1.0). score=clip(z_mom*40*m,-100,100). confidence=min(1,history_days/252), rising with smoothness (low ID/high IR).
strategies.computeProjection: Smooth/high-IR winners have higher, more durable forward returns and less reversal: project a longer holding horizon and higher Sharpe; jumpy winners get a shorter horizon and wider downside variance.
sources: Da, Gurun & Warachka (2014), 'Frog in the Pan: Continuous Information and Momentum', Review of Financial Studies 27(7):2171-2218

strategies.name: Faber 10-Month / 200-Day SMA Timing
strategies.summary: Mebane Faber's tactical timing: hold the asset only when its monthly close is above its 10-month SMA (~200-day), else go to cash. Faber (2006) â€” equity-like returns with bond-like drawdowns by sidestepping major bear markets.
strategies.rules: Monthly (last trading day) compute SMA10m (~200-day SMA of daily closes). Hold if close>SMA; sell to cash (earn risk-free) if below. Re-enter on the next monthly close back above. Long-or-cash, no shorting. Optional 0.5-1% whipsaw band.
strategies.parameters: lookback=10 months (~200d); monthly evaluation; daily proxy 200-day SMA; optional band +-0.5%; long-or-cash
strategies.computeSignal: P=latest close, SMA200=200-day SMA, d=(P-SMA200)/SMA200. score=clamp(d/0.10*100,-100,100) (10%+ above saturates +100, 10%+ below -100). confidence=clamp(0.4+|SMA200 slope over 20d normalized|*k,0.4,0.9) (higher when SMA sloping with the signal). Need >=200 bars else lower confidence.
strategies.computeProjection: When P>SMA200 project the asset's risk premium (trailing mean return annualized, or beta-adjusted market return); when below project the risk-free rate. Risk = realized 200-day stdev; cash state risk ~near-zero. Cuts max drawdown ~in half vs buy-and-hold.
sources: Faber (2007), 'A Quantitative Approach to Tactical Asset Allocation', Journal of Wealth Management, SSRN 962461
https://mebfaber.com/timing-model/

strategies.name: Donchian Channel / Turtle Breakout
strategies.summary: The classic Turtle trend-following system (Dennis & Eckhardt, 1983) built on Donchian channels. Enter long on a new N-day high, exit on the opposite shorter-channel low. System 1: 20-day entry / 10-day exit; System 2: 55-day entry / 20-day exit. Sizing/stops scaled by N (20-day ATR).
strategies.rules: System 1: buy when close>highest high of prior 20 days; exit when close<lowest low of prior 10 days (skip if last 20d breakout was a winner â€” whipsaw filter). System 2: buy on new 55-day high; exit on new 20-day low. Stops=entry-2N; add to winners in 0.5N steps up to 4 units. N=Wilder 20-day ATR, TR=max(high-low,|high-prevClose|,|low-prevClose|).
strategies.parameters: S1 entry 20 / exit 10 (filter on); S2 entry 55 / exit 20 (filter off); N=20-day ATR Wilder; stop=2N; pyramiding 0.5N max 4 units; unit risk 1% equity/N
strategies.computeSignal: HH20/LL10 (S1), HH55/LL20 (S2) from daily highs/lows. State long if a 20/55-day high broke and price hasn't since hit the 10/20-day low. score=+100 on fresh upside breakout, decay toward +40 while long; -100 on fresh downside breakout. Continuous variant: clamp(((P-midline)/(0.5*(HH-LL)))*100,-100,100), midline=(HH20+LL20)/2. confidence=clamp(0.3+min(channel_width_in_N,5)/5*0.6,0.3,0.9), reduced if prior breakout was a loser.
strategies.computeProjection: On a confirmed breakout project continuation at the trailing momentum rate while the 2N stop caps downside; use N (20-day ATR) as the per-day vol scale for sizing.
sources: Curtis Faith (2007), 'Way of the Turtle', McGraw-Hill
'The Original Turtle Trading Rules' â€” https://www.turtletrader.com/rules/

strategies.name: Golden Cross / Death Cross (50/200 SMA)
strategies.summary: Long-term trend regime filter: a Golden Cross (50-day SMA above 200-day) is bullish, a Death Cross (50-day below 200-day) bearish. Widely used by institutions and the press; trims drawdowns more reliably than it boosts raw return.
strategies.rules: SMA50 and SMA200 of daily closes. Golden Cross (long): SMA50 crosses above SMA200. Death Cross (cash): SMA50 crosses below SMA200. Regime persists until the opposite cross. Optionally require SMA200 flat-to-rising to confirm.
strategies.parameters: short MA=50, long MA=200 SMA on daily closes; signal=sign(SMA50-SMA200); need >=200 bars
strategies.computeSignal: spread=(SMA50-SMA200)/SMA200. score=clamp(spread/0.10*100,-100,100). Freshness boost: if a cross occurred within last 5 bars push |score| toward 100. confidence=clamp(0.4+min(|spread|/0.05,1)*0.3+(SMA200 slope agrees?0.2:0),0.4,0.95); low at the crossover (whipsaw), rising as the gap widens.
strategies.computeProjection: Golden-cross regime -> project the asset's trailing positive drift; death-cross -> flat/risk-free (assume exited). Risk = 200-day realized stdev. Roughly matches buy-and-hold returns while reducing drawdown.
sources: Corporate Finance Institute â€” Death Cross â€” https://corporatefinanceinstitute.com/resources/capital-markets/death-cross/
QuantifiedStrategies, 'Does the Death Cross Actually Work?' (65-yr backtest)

strategies.name: Supertrend (ATR Trailing Trend)
strategies.summary: Volatility-adaptive trend indicator (Olivier Seban): an ATR-scaled band trails price; closing above flips the trend bullish, below flips bearish. Default period 10, multiplier 3. Self-adjusts to volatility unlike fixed MAs.
strategies.rules: ATR(10) Wilder; hl2=(high+low)/2; basicUpper=hl2+3*ATR, basicLower=hl2-3*ATR; carry final bands forward. Trend up (bullish, line=finalLower) when close>prevFinalUpper; down when close<prevFinalLower; else carry. Buy on flip up, exit/short on flip down. The line is the trailing stop.
strategies.parameters: ATR period=10, multiplier=3; Wilder ATR; variations period 7-14, mult 1.5-4
strategies.computeSignal: If close>Supertrend line trend up (positive), else negative. score=clamp(((close-Supertrend)/(3*ATR))*100,-100,100) (one full band away saturates +-100). Boost |score| toward 100 on the flip bar. confidence=clamp(0.4+min(ATR_pct_rank,1)*0.2+persistence_bars/20*0.3,0.4,0.9); drops when close hugs the line.
strategies.computeProjection: Project trend continuation while price stays the favorable side; the line is the projected stop, so expected loss if wrong ~(close-Supertrend). Risk scale = 3*ATR. Best as a timing/stop overlay.
sources: Olivier Seban â€” SuperTrend indicator
TradingView Supertrend docs â€” https://www.tradingview.com/support/solutions/43000634738-supertrend/

strategies.name: Moving-Average Ribbon Alignment
strategies.summary: A multi-MA 'ribbon' (10/20/50/100/200) read as one trend gauge: stacked-in-order and fanning apart = strong trend; compressing or crossing = fading/reversing. Generalizes single MA crossovers (Guppy's GMMA).
strategies.rules: SMAs of daily closes for {10,20,50,100,200}. Bullish: SMA10>SMA20>SMA50>SMA100>SMA200 and price>SMA10. Bearish: reverse order. Wider monotonic separation=stronger; compression=weakening. Enter on transition into full alignment, exit on loss of alignment.
strategies.parameters: ribbon {10,20,50,100,200} SMA daily closes (EMA variant for speed); alignment from pairwise ordering; separation as fraction of price
strategies.computeSignal: ordered_score=(bullish-ordered pairs - bearish-ordered pairs)/total pairs in [-1,1]. W=mean(|SMA_i-SMA_{i+1}|)/price (fan width). score=clamp(ordered_score*(0.6+0.4*min(W/0.04,1))*100,-100,100). confidence=clamp(0.3+|ordered_score|*0.4+min(W/0.04,1)*0.25,0.3,0.9); high when fully aligned/fanned, low when tangled.
strategies.computeProjection: Project drift in the alignment direction scaled by fan width (wider=stronger continuation, use trailing return); ribbon compression flags rising reversal probability â€” widen risk (shorter-window vol) when separation collapses.
sources: Daryl Guppy â€” Guppy Multiple Moving Average (GMMA)
QuantifiedStrategies â€” EMA Ribbon strategy â€” https://www.quantifiedstrategies.com/exponential-moving-average-ribbon/

strategies.name: Dual Moving-Average Crossover (Fast/Slow)
strategies.summary: Foundational systematic trend rule: long when a fast MA crosses above a slow MA, exit/short when below. Studied by Brock, Lakonishok & LeBaron (1992); AQR confirms trend premia. A faster sibling of the 50/200 golden cross (e.g. 20/100, 12/26 EMA).
strategies.rules: Fast MA (e.g. 20-day) and slow MA (e.g. 100-day) of daily closes. Long when fastMA>slowMA, exit/short when fastMA<slowMA. EMA variant 12/26 (MACD line>0). Hold until opposite cross. A confirmation band reduces whipsaws.
strategies.parameters: default fast=20, slow=100 SMA (configurable 10/50, 12/26 EMA, 50/200); signal=sign(fastMA-slowMA); EMA alpha=2/(N+1)
strategies.computeSignal: spread=(fastMA-slowMA)/slowMA. score=clamp(spread/0.06*100,-100,100) (tune divisor to the pair). Freshness: boost |score| if crossover within last ~3 bars. confidence=clamp(0.35+min(|spread|/0.03,1)*0.35+(slowMA slope agrees?0.2:0),0.35,0.9); lowest at the crossover.
strategies.computeProjection: Project positive drift while fast>slow (trailing return), flat/risk-free otherwise. Faster pairs catch trends earlier but whipsaw more (lower hit-rate, larger winners). Risk=realized stdev over the slow-MA window.
sources: Brock, Lakonishok & LeBaron (1992), 'Simple Technical Trading Rules', Journal of Finance 47(5):1731-1764
Hurst, Ooi & Pedersen (AQR, 2017), 'A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following Investing'

strategies.name: Connors RSI(2) Mean Reversion
strategies.summary: Larry Connors' 2-period RSI pullback system: buy short-term oversold dips inside an established uptrend (Close>SMA200), exit on the snap-back. ~75-80% historical win rates on equity indices with 1-3 day holds.
strategies.rules: RSI(2) Wilder, SMA200 trend filter, SMA5 exit. Long: Close>SMA200 AND RSI(2)<10 (strongest <5). Exit when Close>SMA5 or RSI(2)>65-70, stop on Close<SMA200. Short mirror: Close<SMA200 and RSI(2)>95; cover on Close<SMA5 or RSI(2)<30. On-close execution, trend filter as risk control.
strategies.parameters: rsi_period=2; oversold_arm=10, strong=5; overbought_arm=90, strong=95; trend_sma=200; exit_sma=5; rsi_exit_long=65; hold 1-5 days
strategies.computeSignal: trend=+1 if Close>SMA200 else -1. If trend==+1 and RSI2<10: signal=+100*max(0,(10-RSI2)/10) (RSI2=5->+50, 0->+100). If trend==-1 and RSI2>90: signal=-100*max(0,(RSI2-90)/10). Outside zones decay toward 0. On Close crossing above SMA5 drive toward 0 (exit). confidence=0.5+0.3*(RSI2<5 or >95)+0.2*(trend agreement); lower on crypto (fat tails).
strategies.computeProjection: Expected 1-5 day return positive and proportional to oversold depth when in-trend; a short-horizon tactical tilt, not a long-run input. Use realized hold-period vol for sizing.
sources: Connors & Alvarez, 'Short Term Trading Strategies That Work' (2009), RSI(2) chapter
StockCharts ChartSchool RSI(2) â€” https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/trading-strategies-and-models/trading-strategies/rsi-2

strategies.name: Connors Cumulative RSI(2)
strategies.summary: Connors & Alvarez refinement (ch. 9): sum the last X RSI(2) readings so a single noisy value cannot trigger; entries require multiple consecutive oversold days. ~88% reported accuracy on SPY (1993-2008) with ~3.7-day holds.
strategies.rules: CumRSI = sum of last X RSI(2) (X=2 or 3). Trend filter Close>SMA200. Long when CumRSI(X=2)<35 (strong <25). Exit when CumRSI>65 or Close>SMA5. Short mirror: Close<SMA200 and CumRSI>165 (X=2). On-close execution.
strategies.parameters: rsi_period=2; cumulative X=2 (alt 3); long_entry_cum<35; long_exit_cum>65; trend_sma=200; exit_sma=5; hold ~3-5 days
strategies.computeSignal: CumRSI=RSI2_t+RSI2_{t-1} (X=2). Long: if Close>SMA200 and CumRSI<35 -> signal=+100*clamp((35-CumRSI)/35,0,1). Short: if Close<SMA200 and CumRSI>165 -> signal=-100*clamp((CumRSI-165)/35,0,1). Else 0. confidence base 0.55 (multi-day confirmation), +0.25 if in strongest decile, +0.2 trend agreement.
strategies.computeProjection: Short-horizon positive expectancy for 3-5 day holds in the in-trend oversold state; tactical overlay, not the long-run mean. Use realized hold-period vol for sizing.
sources: Connors & Alvarez, 'Short Term Trading Strategies That Work' (2009), ch. 9 (Cumulative RSI)
Easycators â€” Cumulative RSI-2 â€” https://easycators.com/thinkscript/cumulative-rsi-2-trading-strategy/

strategies.name: Williams %R Reversion
strategies.summary: Larry Williams' %R bounded oscillator (0 to -100) shows where close sits in the recent high-low range. Mean-reversion: buy oversold (<-80), sell overbought (>-20), exit toward midline. Effectively an inverted Fast Stochastic %K.
strategies.rules: %R=(HighestHigh(14)-Close)/(HighestHigh(14)-LowestLow(14))*-100. Oversold<=-80, overbought>=-20. Long when %R<=-80 then turns up (crosses above -80); exit at -50 or -20. Short mirror. Add SMA200 trend filter (only buy oversold when Close>SMA200) to avoid fighting strong downtrends.
strategies.parameters: wr_period=14 (alt 10); oversold=-80; overbought=-20; exit_mid=-50; optional trend_sma=200
strategies.computeSignal: Compute %R(14) from daily OHLC. score=(-50-%R)/50*100 (%R=-100 -> +100 bullish, 0 -> -100 bearish, -50 -> 0). If Close<SMA200 cap bullish at +40. Full conviction needs a turn-up from below -80. confidence=0.4+0.3*(|%R+50|/50)+0.2*(crossover turn confirmed)+0.1*(trend agreement); crypto -0.1.
strategies.computeProjection: %R range position is a normalized stretch indicator; combine with ATR(14) to translate the snap toward midrange into a return/range estimate. Short-horizon tactical tilt.
sources: Larry Williams (1979), 'How I Made One Million Dollars Last Year Trading Commodities'
StockCharts ChartSchool â€” Williams %R

strategies.name: Stochastic Oscillator (%K/%D) Reversion
strategies.summary: George Lane's Stochastic measures close relative to the recent high-low range. Mean-reversion: buy when %K/%D cross up out of oversold (<20), sell when they cross down out of overbought (>80). Lane stressed crossovers and divergence over bare threshold touches.
strategies.rules: Fast %K=(Close-LowestLow(14))/(HighestHigh(14)-LowestLow(14))*100; Slow %K=SMA(%K,3), %D=SMA(%K_slow,3) (14,3,3). Long: %K crosses above %D while both <20; exit when %K>80 or crosses below %D in overbought. Short mirror. Stronger with divergence; add SMA200 trend bias.
strategies.parameters: k_period=14; k_smooth=3; d_period=3; oversold=20; overbought=80; optional trend_sma=200
strategies.computeSignal: Slow %K, %D(14,3,3) from daily OHLC. score_level=(50-%K)/50*100 (%K=0 -> +100, 100 -> -100). Crossover boost: +15 if %K>%D in oversold zone, -15 if %K<%D in overbought. score=clamp(score_level+adj,-100,100), high conviction only in zone. Cap counter-trend at +-40. confidence=0.4+0.25*(in-zone extreme)+0.2*(crossover confirmed)+0.15*(trend agreement).
strategies.computeProjection: Normalized range position; pair with ATR/realized vol to map the expected reversion move. Short-horizon swing tilt (3-10 day), informs tactical risk-on/off not long-run drift.
sources: George C. Lane â€” Stochastic Oscillator (late 1950s)
StockCharts ChartSchool â€” Stochastic Oscillator (Fast/Slow/Full)

strategies.name: Rolling Z-Score Mean Reversion
strategies.summary: Canonical statistical mean-reversion: standardize price by its rolling mean and stdev; enter when |z|>~2 (price stretched), exit as z reverts toward 0. The engine behind Bollinger %B; the spread version underlies pairs trading. (Distinct from app's 'mean-reversion', which uses a fixed 60-day window with no actionable-band/autocorrelation gating.)
strategies.rules: z_t=(Close_t-SMA(Close,N))/stdev(Close,N), N=20 (alt 50). Long when z<=-2.0 (strong <=-2.5); exit when z>=0 or partial at -0.5. Short when z>=+2.0; cover at 0. Half-life/time stop: exit if no reversion within ~2N days. Only |z|>=2 is actionable.
strategies.parameters: window N=20 (alt 50); entry_z=2.0; strong_z=2.5; exit_z=0.0; time_stop=2N days
strategies.computeSignal: Rolling z of daily close (N=20). signal=clamp(-z/3*100,-100,100) (z=-2 -> +66.7, -3 -> +100, +2 -> -66.7). Only treat |z|>=2 as conviction; |z|<1 noise. confidence=clamp(0.3+0.2*min(|z|/3,1)+0.2*reversion_quality,0,0.9), reversion_quality from negative lag-1 autocorrelation / measured half-life. Trending/positive-autocorr assets (some crypto) get LOWER confidence.
strategies.computeProjection: z gives stdev-units of dislocation; expected reversion ~ z*rolling_stdev toward the mean. Rolling stdev is the position-sizing risk unit; AR(1) half-life sets the expected holding horizon.
sources: Gatev, Goetzmann & Rouwenhorst (2006), 'Pairs Trading', Review of Financial Studies â€” https://doi.org/10.1093/rfs/hhj020
Ernest Chan, 'Algorithmic Trading' (Wiley, 2013) â€” z-score/half-life

strategies.name: Keltner Channel Mean Reversion (ATR bands)
strategies.summary: Keltner Channels (Chester Keltner, 1960; Raschke/Colby refinements) plot ATR-based bands around an EMA. In range-bound markets, outer-band touches flag overshoots that revert to the middle EMA. Because bands use ATR not close-stdev, they pair with Bollinger to distinguish reversion vs breakout.
strategies.rules: Middle=EMA20; band=ATR(10)*2.0; UpperKC=EMA20+2*ATR, LowerKC=EMA20-2*ATR. Long when Close<=LowerKC and momentum stalls; exit at EMA20. Short at UpperKC; cover at EMA20. Squeeze guard: if Bollinger(20,2) is INSIDE the Keltner channel (TTM-squeeze), suppress reversion (breakout likely). Trend filter for longs (Close>SMA200).
strategies.parameters: ema_period=20; atr_period=10 (alt 14); atr_mult=2.0 (alt 1.5-3.0); exit=EMA20; squeeze guard=BB(20,2) inside KC(20,2*ATR)
strategies.computeSignal: EMA20, ATR(10) from daily OHLC; p=(Close-EMA20)/(2*ATR) (~-1 at lower band, +1 at upper). score=clamp(-p*100,-100,100). Squeeze guard: if BB width<KC width scale score by 0.3 and flag breakout regime. Trend gate caps counter-trend at +-40. confidence=0.4+0.3*min(|p|,1.5)/1.5+0.2*(1-squeeze_flag)+0.1*(trend agreement).
strategies.computeProjection: ATR-scaled distance from the EMA is a vol-normalized dislocation; expected reversion ~(EMA20-Close) with risk=ATR. Squeeze flag flips the projection from reversion to breakout-vol expansion.
sources: Chester W. Keltner (1960), 'How To Make Money in Commodities'
StockCharts ChartSchool â€” Keltner Channels

strategies.name: Commodity Channel Index (CCI) Reversion
strategies.summary: Donald Lambert's CCI (1980) measures how far typical price is from its MA in mean-deviation units, scaled so ~70-80% of values fall within +-100. Reversion variant: fade readings beyond +-100, targeting a return toward 0.
strategies.rules: TP=(High+Low+Close)/3; CCI=(TP-SMA(TP,20))/(0.015*MeanDeviation(TP,20)). Above +100 overbought, below -100 oversold. Long when CCI<-100 then turns up (crosses above -100); exit at 0/+100. Short when CCI>+100 then turns down; cover at 0. Extreme bands +-200 for stronger entries. SMA200 trend filter to avoid fading strong trends.
strategies.parameters: cci_period=20; constant=0.015; oversold=-100 (strong -200); overbought=+100 (strong +200); exit=0; optional trend_sma=200
strategies.computeSignal: CCI(20) from daily OHLC typical price. score=clamp(-CCI/200*100,-100,100) (CCI=-200 -> +100, -100 -> +50, +200 -> -100). High conviction only |CCI|>100; require a cross-back through +-100 for full score else 60%. Trend gate caps counter-trend at +-40. confidence=0.4+0.3*min(|CCI|/200,1)+0.2*(cross-back confirmed)+0.1*(trend agreement); crypto -0.1.
strategies.computeProjection: CCI is normalized stretch in mean-deviation units; expected reversion ~CCI*0.015*MeanDeviation back toward the SMA(TP). MeanDeviation/ATR sizes risk. Short-horizon tactical tilt.
sources: Donald R. Lambert (1980), 'Commodity Channel Index', Commodities Magazine
StockCharts ChartSchool â€” CCI

strategies.name: Bollinger Band Squeeze (Volatility Breakout)
strategies.summary: Bollinger's 'The Squeeze': BandWidth contracting to a multi-month low signals a low-volatility coil that precedes high-volatility expansion ('low vol begets high vol'). The first band break after a squeeze signals direction; beware the 'head fake'.
strategies.rules: BandWidth=(UpperBB-LowerBB)/MiddleBB using SMA(20),2 std. Squeeze=BandWidth at/near its 125-day (6-month) low. Breakout long when, in/just after a squeeze, Close>UpperBB on rising volume; short when Close<LowerBB. Head-fake guard: if a break reverses back inside within a few bars, trade the opposite direction. Exit via opposite/middle band.
strategies.parameters: bb_period=20; bb_stdev_mult=2.0; bandwidth_lookback=125; squeeze=BandWidth<=20th percentile; breakout=close beyond band + volume>1.5x avg(20)
strategies.computeSignal: BandWidth and its 125-day percentile. squeeze_strength=1-percentile(BandWidth). signal=+100*squeeze_strength when Close>UpperBB; -100*squeeze_strength when Close<LowerBB; in squeeze with no break -> 0 (flag 'armed'). Multiply by min(1,vol/(1.5*avgvol20)). Head-fake: reverse within 3 bars -> opposite sign at 60% magnitude. confidence=0.4+0.3*squeeze_strength+0.2*volume_confirm+0.1*(break aligns with SMA200).
strategies.computeProjection: Squeeze identifies low-realized-vol -> projected vol expansion (widen risk bands once a break occurs). Use ATR(14) at the break for stop distance and expected-move sizing; expected move often >= prior squeeze BandWidth.
sources: John Bollinger (2001), 'Bollinger on Bollinger Bands' â€” The Squeeze & Volatility Breakout Method I
StockCharts ChartSchool â€” Bollinger Band Squeeze

strategies.name: Ray Dalio All-Weather (Risk Parity)
strategies.summary: Bridgewater's strategic allocation balancing risk (not dollars) across economic 'seasons'. Retail version ~30% stocks, 40% long Treasuries, 15% intermediate Treasuries, 7.5% gold, 7.5% commodities â€” the heavy bond weight equalizes risk contributions since bonds are ~1/3 as volatile as equities.
strategies.rules: Map the universe to All-Weather buckets: 30% growth (equities+SPY/QQQ), 40% long-bond proxy, 15% intermediate-bond proxy, 7.5% GLD, 7.5% commodity/crypto. No bond ETF here: use risk-free/cash as the bond proxy (flag low confidence on bond legs). Rebalance when any sleeve drifts >5% absolute or annually. Signal is over/underweight vs target.
strategies.parameters: weights 30/40/15/7.5/7.5; bond sleeve proxied by risk-free/cash; rebalance >5% drift or annual; vols from 5y daily returns annualized
strategies.computeSignal: Allocation overlay: score=clamp(100*(target_weight-current_weight)/target_weight,-100,100) (under-target=positive/accumulate, over=trim). Assets not in a bucket -> 0. confidence=0.7 for SPY/QQQ/GLD legs, 0.4 for the bond sleeve (proxied), 0.2 for unmapped names.
strategies.computeProjection: Blend each sleeve's long-run risk premium (equities>commodities/gold>bonds>cash); portfolio vol from the covariance of sleeve daily returns; All-Weather targets ~10-12% vol with shallower drawdowns than 60/40 in inflation/deflation shocks.
sources: Ray Dalio / Bridgewater â€” All-Weather principles
Tony Robbins, 'Money: Master the Game' (2014) â€” retail All-Weather weights

strategies.name: Risk Parity / Inverse-Volatility Weighting
strategies.summary: Equalize each asset's risk contribution by weighting inversely to volatility (naive risk parity), so low-vol assets get more capital and high-vol assets less. Maillard, Roncalli & Teiletche (2010); the diversification engine behind All-Weather.
strategies.rules: w_i proportional to 1/sigma_i, normalized to sum 1 (naive risk parity). Full risk parity solves for equal marginal risk contributions via the covariance matrix. Rebalance monthly/quarterly. Lower-vol assets earn higher weight; the 'signal' per asset is its inverse-vol weight vs equal-weight.
strategies.parameters: sigma_i=annualized stdev of daily log returns (252); w_i ~ (1/sigma_i)/sum(1/sigma_j); rebalance monthly/quarterly
strategies.computeSignal: sigma_i=sqrt(252)*std(daily log returns). iv_w=(1/sigma_i)/sum over universe; eq_w=1/N. score=clamp(100*(iv_w-eq_w)/eq_w,-100,100) (low-vol assets above equal-weight -> positive/accumulate, high-vol -> negative/trim). confidence=clamp(0.5+0.3*min(1,history_days/252),0,0.85); a pure risk-allocation overlay, not a return forecast.
strategies.computeProjection: No drift claim per asset; portfolio vol from the inverse-vol-weighted covariance is lower and more stable than equal- or cap-weight. Use realized sigma as the forward vol input; expect smoother drawdowns.
sources: Maillard, Roncalli & Teiletche (2010), 'The Properties of Equally Weighted Risk Contribution Portfolios', Journal of Portfolio Management
Qian (2005), 'Risk Parity Portfolios', PanAgora

strategies.name: Minimum-Variance Portfolio
strategies.summary: Solve for the portfolio with the lowest total variance regardless of expected return (the leftmost point of the efficient frontier). The minimum-variance and low-volatility anomaly portfolios have historically delivered high risk-adjusted returns. Clarke, de Silva & Thorley (2006).
strategies.rules: Minimize w'Sigma w subject to sum(w)=1 (long-only adds w>=0), using the covariance matrix of daily returns over the 24-asset universe. Low-beta, low-vol, low-correlation assets get the highest weights. Rebalance monthly/quarterly. Per-asset signal is its min-variance weight vs equal-weight.
strategies.parameters: Sigma=sample covariance of daily returns (shrinkage recommended, Ledoit-Wolf); long-only w>=0, sum(w)=1; rebalance monthly/quarterly
strategies.computeSignal: Estimate Sigma (Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage on daily returns). Solve min-variance weights w*. score=clamp(100*(w*_i-eq_w)/eq_w,-100,100) (above equal-weight -> accumulate, near-zero weight -> avoid). Proxy when full optimizer unavailable: rank by combined low beta + low vol + low average correlation. confidence=clamp(0.45+0.3*min(1,history_days/252),0,0.8); estimation error in Sigma caps confidence.
strategies.computeProjection: Captures the low-volatility anomaly: minimum-variance names project competitive returns at materially lower vol and drawdown. Use the optimized portfolio variance as the forward risk input.
sources: Clarke, de Silva & Thorley (2006), 'Minimum-Variance Portfolios in the US Equity Market', Journal of Portfolio Management
Ledoit & Wolf (2004), 'Honey, I Shrunk the Sample Covariance Matrix'

strategies.name: Low-Volatility Anomaly
strategies.summary: Low-volatility (and low-beta) stocks have historically earned higher risk-adjusted â€” and often higher absolute â€” returns than high-volatility stocks, contradicting CAPM. Ang, Hodrick, Xing & Zhang (2006); Baker, Bradley & Wurgler (2011).
strategies.rules: Rank the universe by trailing realized volatility (or beta). Long the lowest-vol tertile/quintile, avoid the highest. The premium is strongest cross-sectionally. Rebalance monthly/quarterly. Often combined with a quality overlay to avoid value traps.
strategies.parameters: metric=trailing 252-day annualized vol (or beta from factor series); long lowest tertile, avoid highest; rebalance monthly/quarterly
strategies.computeSignal: sigma_i=sqrt(252)*std(daily log returns) over ~252 days. Percentile-rank across the universe (low vol = high rank). score=clip((low_vol_pct-50)*2,-100,100) i.e. lowest-vol -> +100, highest -> -100. Optionally blend with beta percentile. confidence=clamp(0.5+0.3*min(1,history_days/252),0,0.8).
strategies.computeProjection: Low-vol names project competitive expected returns with lower drawdown; tilt the risk estimate down for low-vol names and up for high-vol names. The anomaly is a risk-reducer that historically did not sacrifice return.
sources: Ang, Hodrick, Xing & Zhang (2006), 'The Cross-Section of Volatility and Expected Returns', Journal of Finance 61(1)
Baker, Bradley & Wurgler (2011), 'Benchmarks as Limits to Arbitrage', Financial Analysts Journal

strategies.name: Betting Against Beta (BAB)
strategies.summary: Frazzini & Pedersen (2014): low-beta assets earn higher risk-adjusted returns than high-beta assets because leverage-constrained investors bid up high-beta. BAB goes long (leveraged) low-beta and short (de-leveraged) high-beta, beta-neutral.
strategies.rules: Rank the universe by beta (supplied per asset, or estimated vs the market factor). Long low-beta, short high-beta, scaling each leg to beta-1 (long leg levered up, short leg levered down) so the spread is beta-neutral. Rebalance monthly. The signal per asset is its beta rank â€” low beta bullish.
strategies.parameters: beta from supplied per-asset beta (or regress returns on market factor); long low-beta, short high-beta; lever each leg to beta=1; rebalance monthly
strategies.computeSignal: Use the asset's beta (or estimate via regression on the market factor). Cross-sectionally rank beta; low beta = bullish. score=clip((low_beta_pct-50)*2,-100,100) where low_beta_pct=1-percentile(beta). Equivalent: score=clip(-(beta-universe_median_beta)/universe_beta_std*40,-100,100). confidence=clamp(0.5+0.3*min(1,history_days/252),0,0.8); higher when beta is well-estimated (long history, high R^2).
strategies.computeProjection: Low-beta names project a positive risk-adjusted tilt and lower systematic risk; use the lower beta directly to reduce projected market-driven variance. The BAB premium is the long-low/short-high spread.
sources: Frazzini & Pedersen (2014), 'Betting Against Beta', Journal of Financial Economics 111(1):1-25 â€” https://www.aqr.com/Insights/Research/Journal-Article/Betting-Against-Beta

strategies.name: Harry Browne Permanent Portfolio
strategies.summary: Harry Browne's Permanent Portfolio: 25% stocks, 25% long-term bonds, 25% cash, 25% gold â€” one asset thrives in each economic regime (prosperity, deflation, recession, inflation). Simple, robust, low-drawdown buy-and-hold-and-rebalance.
strategies.rules: Equal 25% to four sleeves: stocks (SPY/QQQ/equities), long bonds (proxy via risk-free/cash duration â€” flag proxy), cash (risk-free), gold (GLD). Rebalance back to 25% each when any sleeve drifts outside a 15-35% band, or annually. Static target; per-asset signal is over/underweight vs the 25% target within its sleeve.
strategies.parameters: weights 25/25/25/25 (stocks/long bonds/cash/gold); rebalance band 15-35% or annual; bond sleeve proxied by cash/risk-free (flag)
strategies.computeSignal: Allocation overlay: score=clamp(100*(target_weight-current_weight)/target_weight,-100,100) within each mapped sleeve (under -> accumulate, over -> trim). SPY/QQQ map to stocks, GLD to gold, cash/risk-free to cash+bond sleeves. Unmapped names -> 0. confidence=0.7 for stock/gold/cash legs that map cleanly, 0.4 for the long-bond proxy, 0.2 for unmapped.
strategies.computeProjection: Blend the four sleeves' long-run premia; portfolio vol from sleeve covariance is low (gold/bonds/cash diversify equities). Historically ~half the drawdown of all-equity with steady real returns.
sources: Harry Browne, 'Fail-Safe Investing' (1999)
Craig Rowland & J.M. Lawson, 'The Permanent Portfolio' (Wiley, 2012)

strategies.name: Volatility Targeting
strategies.summary: Scale exposure inversely to forecast volatility to hold portfolio risk at a constant target, lowering exposure when vol spikes and raising it when calm. Improves risk-adjusted returns and curbs drawdowns since vol is persistent and clusters before crashes (Moreira & Muir, 2017).
strategies.rules: position weight = target_vol / forecast_vol, capped at a max leverage. forecast_vol from EWMA/GARCH of daily returns. As realized/forecast vol rises, cut exposure; as it falls, add. Rebalance daily/weekly. Per-asset signal compares current vol regime to its own long-run average.
strategies.parameters: target_vol=10-15% annualized; forecast_vol=EWMA(delta=0.94) or GARCH(1,1); max leverage cap (e.g. 1.5-2x); rebalance daily/weekly
strategies.computeSignal: forecast_vol=sqrt(252*EWMA(r_daily^2,0.94)) (or GARCH(1,1)). long_run_vol=annualized stdev over full history. ratio=long_run_vol/forecast_vol (>1 calm = scale up, <1 stressed = scale down). score=clip((ratio-1)*100,-100,100) (calm regime mildly bullish on a risk-adjusted basis, vol spike bearish). confidence=clamp(0.4+0.3*min(1,history_days/252),0,0.8).
strategies.computeProjection: Primary use is sizing: target_weight=target_vol/forecast_vol. Project the asset's drift unchanged but set forward vol to the targeted level; vol targeting compresses tail risk and stabilizes the Sharpe across regimes.
sources: Moreira & Muir (2017), 'Volatility-Managed Portfolios', Journal of Finance 72(4)
Harvey et al. (2018), 'The Impact of Volatility Targeting', Journal of Portfolio Management

strategies.name: Calendar Seasonality (Sell-in-May / Halloween + TOM)
strategies.summary: Well-documented calendar effects: the Halloween / 'Sell in May' indicator (Nov-Apr returns historically far exceed May-Oct) and the turn-of-month effect (returns cluster around the last/first few trading days). Bouman & Jacobsen (2002); Ariel (1987).
strategies.rules: Halloween: overweight equities Nov-Apr, underweight/defensive May-Oct. Turn-of-month: tilt long in the window from the last trading day of the month through the first ~3 trading days of the next month. Combine: strongest bullish when in the Nov-Apr window AND in the turn-of-month window. Derive month/day from the daily price series dates.
strategies.parameters: Halloween window = Nov 1 - Apr 30 (favorable); TOM window = last trading day + first 3 trading days; effects are universe-wide (apply to equities/ETFs)
strategies.computeSignal: From the date of the latest bar: halloween_score=+40 if month in {Nov..Apr} else -40. tom_score=+40 if within the turn-of-month window (last trading day or first 3 of the month) else 0. score=clamp(halloween_score+tom_score,-100,100). Scale magnitude by the asset's historical realized seasonal edge if estimable from the 5y series. confidence=0.35-0.5 (calendar effects are real but modest and time-varying); higher when both windows align.
strategies.computeProjection: Add a small additive seasonal drift to expected return for the favorable window (Nov-Apr / turn-of-month) and a small subtraction in the unfavorable window; the effect is a low-magnitude timing tilt, neutral on vol.
sources: Bouman & Jacobsen (2002), 'The Halloween Indicator, Sell in May and Go Away', American Economic Review 92(5)
Ariel (1987), 'A Monthly Effect in Stock Returns', Journal of Financial Economics

strategies.name: Pairs Trading (Statistical Arbitrage)
strategies.summary: Market-neutral relative-value: find two historically co-moving assets, trade the spread when it diverges (short the rich, long the cheap), exit when it converges. Gatev, Goetzmann & Rouwenhorst (2006) document persistent profits.
strategies.rules: For a candidate pair (A,B): estimate hedge ratio beta via rolling regression of log(A) on log(B); spread=log(A)-beta*log(B). z-score the spread. Enter when z>=+2 (short A/long B) or z<=-2 (long A/short B); exit at z~0. Require the pair to be cointegrated/correlated on the 5y daily series (e.g. correlation>0.8, ADF on spread). Time-stop if no convergence within ~2N days.
strategies.parameters: hedge ratio=rolling OLS log(A)~log(B); spread z-window N=20-60; entry_z=2.0; exit_z=0.0; pair filter correlation>0.8 / cointegrated; time_stop=2N
strategies.computeSignal: Per asset, find its best-correlated partner in the universe; build spread=log(price_self)-beta*log(price_partner); z=spread z-score over N. signal for the self-asset=clamp(-z/2.5*100,-100,100) (self cheap relative to partner, z<=-2 -> bullish on self). confidence=clamp(0.3+0.3*pair_correlation+0.2*min(|z|/2.5,1),0,0.85); zero confidence if no partner with correlation>0.7 (no tradable pair).
strategies.computeProjection: Market-neutral: project the spread reverting to zero, so expected self-return ~ -z*spread_stdev relative to the partner; portfolio-level vol is low (hedged). Half-life of the spread sets the holding horizon.
sources: Gatev, Goetzmann & Rouwenhorst (2006), 'Pairs Trading: Performance of a Relative-Value Arbitrage Rule', Review of Financial Studies â€” https://doi.org/10.1093/rfs/hhj020
Ernest Chan, 'Algorithmic Trading' (Wiley, 2013)

strategies.name: Ichimoku Kinko Hyo (Cloud)
strategies.summary: Goichi Hosoda's all-in-one trend system: Tenkan/Kijun lines, a forward-projected Kumo (cloud) of leading spans, and the lagging Chikou span give trend, support/resistance, and momentum from one chart. Price above the cloud is bullish, below is bearish.
strategies.rules: Tenkan=(9-period high+low)/2; Kijun=(26-period high+low)/2; SpanA=(Tenkan+Kijun)/2 plotted 26 ahead; SpanB=(52-period high+low)/2 plotted 26 ahead; Chikou=close plotted 26 back. Bullish: price>cloud, Tenkan>Kijun, SpanA>SpanB (green cloud), Chikou above price 26 bars ago. Bearish: the mirror. Entry on a Tenkan/Kijun cross confirmed by cloud position.
strategies.parameters: Tenkan=9, Kijun=26, SpanB=52, displacement=26 (uses daily high/low/close)
strategies.computeSignal: Compute the five lines from daily OHLC. Component votes: +1 each for price>cloud, Tenkan>Kijun, SpanA>SpanB, close>close[26] (Chikou); -1 each for the bearish mirror. raw=sum/4 in [-1,1]. Cloud-distance strength W=clip((close-cloud_mid)/(cloud_thickness or price*0.05),-1,1). score=clamp(raw*(0.6+0.4*|W|)*100,-100,100). confidence=clamp(0.4+0.3*|raw|+0.2*min(cloud_thickness/(price*0.03),1),0.4,0.9); higher with thicker cloud and full alignment.
strategies.computeProjection: Project trend continuation in the cloud direction; the cloud edges are projected support/resistance and stop levels. Thicker cloud = stronger expected continuation and wider support buffer; thin cloud = weaker, more reversal-prone.
sources: Goichi Hosoda â€” Ichimoku Kinko Hyo
StockCharts ChartSchool â€” Ichimoku Cloud â€” https://chartschool.stockcharts.com/table-of-contents/technical-indicators-and-overlays/technical-overlays/ichimoku-cloud

strategies.name: ADX Trend-Strength Filter (Wilder DMI)
strategies.summary: Wilder's Average Directional Index measures trend STRENGTH (not direction); +DI/-DI give direction. ADX>25 confirms a trend worth following, ADX<20 flags a choppy range where trend signals fail and reversion works. A regime gauge that gates other strategies.
strategies.rules: Compute +DM/-DM, TR, then +DI=100*EMA(+DM)/ATR, -DI=100*EMA(-DM)/ATR, DX=100*|+DI - -DI|/(+DI + -DI), ADX=Wilder-smoothed DX (period 14). Direction: bullish when +DI>-DI, bearish when -DI>+DI. Strength: only act on trend signals when ADX>25; treat ADX<20 as range (favor mean-reversion). Rising ADX = strengthening trend.
strategies.parameters: period=14 (Wilder); trend threshold ADX>25; range threshold ADX<20; +DI/-DI for direction
strategies.computeSignal: Compute ADX(14), +DI, -DI from daily OHLC. direction=sign(+DI - -DI). strength=clip((ADX-20)/30,0,1) (ADX=20 -> 0, 50 -> 1). score=clamp(direction*strength*100,-100,100) (strong directional trend -> strong signed score; weak ADX -> near 0). confidence=clamp(0.4+0.4*strength+0.1*(rising ADX?1:0),0.4,0.9).
strategies.computeProjection: ADX is best used as a regime multiplier on other strategies: scale trend-strategy expected returns by strength and mean-reversion strategies by (1-strength). High ADX projects trend continuation; low ADX projects range-bound chop.
sources: J. Welles Wilder (1978), 'New Concepts in Technical Trading Systems' â€” ADX/DMI
StockCharts ChartSchool â€” ADX/DMI

strategies.name: On-Balance Volume Trend Confirmation
strategies.summary: Joe Granville's On-Balance Volume cumulates signed volume (add volume on up days, subtract on down days) to confirm price trends and spot divergences: OBV making new highs with price confirms the trend; OBV diverging warns of a reversal.
strategies.rules: OBV_t = OBV_{t-1} + volume if close>prevClose, - volume if close<prevClose, else unchanged. Confirmation: rising OBV with rising price = healthy uptrend (bullish); falling OBV with rising price = bearish divergence (distribution). Use the OBV slope and OBV vs its own moving average; a bullish OBV breakout often leads price.
strategies.parameters: OBV cumulative signed volume; trend via OBV slope over ~20-50 days or OBV vs its 20-day EMA; divergence vs price over the same window
strategies.computeSignal: Build OBV from daily close+volume. obv_slope=normalized slope of OBV over last ~20 days (e.g. (OBV-OBV[-20])/mean|OBV change|). price_slope=(close/close[-20]-1). Agreement: score=clamp(sign(obv_slope)*min(|obv_slope_z|,2.5)*40,-100,100); apply a divergence penalty: if sign(obv_slope)!=sign(price_slope), halve magnitude and flag divergence. confidence=clamp(0.35+0.3*min(|obv_slope_z|/2.5,1)+0.2*(agreement?1:0),0,0.85). Requires volume (available in OHLCV).
strategies.computeProjection: OBV confirms or questions the price trend rather than forecasting magnitude: when OBV agrees, raise confidence in the price-trend projection; on bearish divergence, widen downside risk and shorten the expected trend horizon.
sources: Joseph Granville (1963), 'Granville's New Key to Stock Market Profits' â€” OBV
StockCharts ChartSchool â€” On Balance Volume (OBV)

strategies.name: Reverse-Engineered Implied Growth (DCF/DDM)
strategies.summary: Invert the valuation model: instead of computing fair value from an assumed growth rate, solve for the growth rate the current price implies and compare it to the firm's achievable growth. A market-implied growth far above what fundamentals support is bearish; well below is bullish (margin of safety).
strategies.rules: From the Gordon/earnings-yield identity, implied_growth = required_return - (cash_flow_per_share/price). Use fcf_per_share (or eps) as the per-share cash flow, required_return from CAPM (rf + beta*premium). Compare implied_growth to a sustainable-growth ceiling g_sustainable = ROE*(1-payout) (or capped revenue_growth). Bullish when implied_growth << g_sustainable (market underpricing growth); bearish when implied_growth >> g_sustainable (priced for unrealistic growth).
strategies.parameters: required_return=CAPM (rf+beta*premium), clamped 0.05-0.20; cash flow=fcf_per_share (fallback eps); g_sustainable=ROE*(1-payout) capped at revenue_growth+5%
strategies.computeSignal: req=clamp(rf+beta*premium,0.05,0.20); cf=fcf_per_share>0?fcf_per_share:eps.
