If cf<=0 or price<=0 -> score 0, conf 0.15. implied_g=req-cf/price. g_sust=clamp((eps/bvps)*(1-clamp(dividend/eps,0,1)),-0.05,0.30). gap=g_sust-implied_g. score=clamp(squash(gap,scale=0.05),-100,100) (market implies far less growth than achievable -> bullish). confidence=0.45 (single-snapshot growth estimate), up to 0.6 when fcf and bvps clean. Crypto/ETF N/A.
strategies.computeProjection: The gap between sustainable and implied growth is a margin-of-safety proxy: feed it into expected mean-reversion of valuation toward fair multiples; pairs naturally with the existing DCF/DDM models as a cross-check.
sources: Mauboussin & Rappaport, 'Expectations Investing' (2001) â€” market-implied expectations
Gordon (1959) growth model inverted; Damodaran on implied growth

projectionMethods.name: Regime-Aware Drift Blending
projectionMethods.summary: Replace a single historical mean drift with a drift that depends on the current market regime (bull/bear/high-vol/low-vol), detected from trend filters and volatility state, so the expected-return input adapts instead of averaging across opposite regimes.
projectionMethods.howToApply: Classify the current regime per asset and for SPY: (a) trend state from the 200-day SMA (price>SMA200 = bull, below = bear) and the golden/death-cross spread; (b) vol state from EWMA/GARCH vol vs its long-run average. Maintain a small drift estimate conditional on regime (e.g. mean daily log return computed only over historical days in the same regime, or a Markov-switching two-state mean). Final drift = regime-conditional mean, shrunk toward the unconditional mean by sample size. Feed this drift into the GBM/horizon projection instead of the flat mean.
sources: Hamilton (1989), 'A New Approach to the Economic Analysis of Nonstationary Time Series', Econometrica (Markov regime-switching)
Ang & Bekaert (2002), 'Regime Switches in Interest Rates'

projectionMethods.name: GARCH(1,1) Conditional Volatility for Forward Vol
projectionMethods.summary: Use the GARCH(1,1) conditional-volatility forecast (already implemented in the app) as the forward sigma in every projection, rather than trailing realized stdev, so confidence intervals widen ahead of clustered-volatility episodes and tighten in calm regimes.
projectionMethods.howToApply: Fit GARCH(1,1) on daily returns; forecast h-step-ahead variance and annualize. Use this forecast_vol as the sigma in Monte Carlo paths and in analytic horizon bands. For multi-horizon projections, iterate the GARCH variance recursion forward to each horizon (vol mean-reverts toward the long-run level omega/(1-alpha-beta)). Blend with EWMA(delta=0.94) as a robustness check when the GARCH fit is unstable (alpha+beta>=1).
sources: Bollerslev (1986), 'Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity', Journal of Econometrics
JP Morgan RiskMetrics (1996) â€” EWMA

projectionMethods.name: Ensemble Blending of Strategy Drifts
projectionMethods.summary: Combine the per-strategy expected-return implications into one blended drift using confidence-weighted (and optionally category-diversified) averaging, so no single model dominates and offsetting signals net out â€” a model-averaging approach that reduces forecast variance.
projectionMethods.howToApply: For each strategy that emits a forward drift, weight its drift contribution by its confidence times a category weight (cap any one family so e.g. the many trend models don't swamp value/quality). Compute blended_drift = sum(w_i * drift_i)/sum(w_i). Optionally shrink the blend toward the CAPM/factor-implied drift (a sensible prior). Track the historical hit-rate of each strategy and let weights adapt (Bayesian/exponential-weights model averaging).
sources: Bates & Granger (1969), 'The Combination of Forecasts'
Timmermann (2006), 'Forecast Combinations', Handbook of Economic Forecasting

projectionMethods.name: Bull/Base/Bear Scenario Fan
projectionMethods.summary: Project three explicit scenarios per horizon â€” bear (e.g. 10th-percentile drift), base (blended drift), bull (90th-percentile drift) â€” instead of a single point, giving users an interpretable range and the engine a structured way to express uncertainty.
projectionMethods.howToApply: For each horizon, set base = blended regime-aware drift. Derive bear/bull by shifting the drift by +-k*forecast_vol/sqrt(horizon) (k chosen for the 10/90 percentiles, ~1.28 for a normal, larger for fat tails) or by taking the 10th/50th/90th percentile of the Monte Carlo terminal distribution. Report the three terminal prices/returns and attach probabilities (e.g. 25/50/25). Scenario weights can themselves be regime-conditional (higher bear weight in a death-cross regime).
sources: Damodaran, 'Investment Valuation' â€” scenario analysis
CFA Institute curriculum â€” scenario and sensitivity analysis

projectionMethods.name: Fat-Tailed / Bootstrapped Confidence Intervals
projectionMethods.summary: Build horizon confidence intervals from a Student-t or block-bootstrap of historical returns rather than a Gaussian, so the bands reflect the heavy tails and autocorrelation observed in real (especially crypto) return series.
projectionMethods.howToApply: Option A (parametric fat tails): fit a Student-t to daily returns (estimate degrees of freedom ~4-8), simulate Monte Carlo paths with t-distributed shocks scaled to the GARCH vol. Option B (nonparametric): block-bootstrap historical daily returns (block length ~10-20 days to preserve autocorrelation/vol clustering), compound to each horizon, and read the empirical 5/25/75/95 percentiles. Use the bootstrap percentiles directly as the projection bands; they need no distributional assumption.
sources: Politis & Romano (1994), 'The Stationary Bootstrap', JASA
Cont (2001), 'Empirical Properties of Asset Returns: Stylized Facts', Quantitative Finance

projectionMethods.name: Expected Shortfall (CVaR) Downside Projection
projectionMethods.summary: Report expected shortfall (mean loss beyond the VaR threshold) alongside each horizon projection so the downside is characterized by the average tail loss, not just a single quantile â€” more coherent and more conservative than VaR.
projectionMethods.howToApply: From the Monte Carlo terminal distribution (or bootstrap) at each horizon, compute VaR_95 = -5th percentile of returns and ES_95 = -mean of returns below the 5th percentile. Attach ES to the bear scenario and to the risk score. For sizing, prefer ES over VaR (it is sub-additive/coherent). Annualize or horizon-scale consistently with the projection horizon. Cross-check against the existing historical VaR/CVaR model.
sources: Artzner, Delbaen, Eber & Heath (1999), 'Coherent Measures of Risk', Mathematical Finance
Rockafellar & Uryasev (2000), 'Optimization of Conditional Value-at-Risk', Journal of Risk

projectionMethods.name: Volatility-Scaled (Risk-Targeted) Expected Returns
projectionMethods.summary: Express and size each projection at a constant target volatility by scaling the position to target_vol/forecast_vol, so expected returns are comparable across assets and the portfolio Sharpe is stabilized across regimes.
projectionMethods.howToApply: Compute forecast_vol (EWMA/GARCH). Scale each asset's projected drift exposure by target_vol/forecast_vol (capped at a max leverage). Report both the raw drift and the risk-targeted drift. At the portfolio level, re-scale aggregate exposure so total forecast vol equals the target. This integrates directly with the TSMOM, vol-target, and risk-parity strategies in the catalog.
sources: Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012), 'Time Series Momentum', JFE
Moreira & Muir (2017), 'Volatility-Managed Portfolios', Journal of Finance

projectionMethods.name: Shrinkage of Drift Estimates (James-Stein / CAPM prior)
projectionMethods.summary: Shrink noisy sample-mean drift estimates toward a structured prior (the cross-sectional mean or the CAPM/factor-implied return), which provably reduces estimation error and prevents the projection from chasing high but unreliable historical means.
projectionMethods.howToApply: Compute each asset's sample drift and its standard error (sigma/sqrt(n)). Set the prior = CAPM expected return (rf + beta*market_premium) or the universe cross-sectional mean. shrunk_drift = w*sample_drift + (1-w)*prior, with w increasing in sample size and decreasing in estimation variance (James-Stein-style). Use the shrunk drift as the projection input; report the shrinkage weight as a confidence cue.
sources: James & Stein (1961), 'Estimation with Quadratic Loss'
Jorion (1986), 'Bayes-Stein Estimation for Portfolio Analysis', JFQA
Black & Litterman (1992) â€” equilibrium prior for expected returns

backtestMetrics.name: CAGR
backtestMetrics.summary: Compound annual growth rate of the equity curve: the constant annual rate that grows the starting value to the ending value over N trading days. The headline return metric.

backtestMetrics.name: Sharpe Ratio
backtestMetrics.formula: Sharpe = sqrt(252) * mean(r_t - rf_daily) / std(r_t - rf_daily)
backtestMetrics.summary: Annualized excess return per unit of total volatility of the daily strategy returns. The primary risk-adjusted return metric; >1 is good, >2 strong.

backtestMetrics.name: Sortino Ratio
backtestMetrics.formula: Sortino = sqrt(252) * mean(r_t - rf_daily) / sqrt(mean(min(r_t - MAR, 0)^2))
backtestMetrics.summary: Like Sharpe but divides only by downside deviation (returns below the minimum acceptable return / risk-free), rewarding strategies that limit losses rather than penalizing upside volatility.

backtestMetrics.name: Calmar Ratio
backtestMetrics.summary: Annualized return divided by the worst peak-to-trough drawdown over the period; measures return earned per unit of worst-case pain. Higher is better; >0.5 is respectable.

backtestMetrics.name: Maximum Drawdown
backtestMetrics.summary: The largest peak-to-trough decline of the equity curve, expressed as a negative percentage. The key tail-risk / worst-case-loss measure for an investor's tolerance.

backtestMetrics.name: Ulcer Index
backtestMetrics.formula: UI = sqrt( mean( D_t^2 ) ), where D_t = 100*(Equity_t/cummax(Equity)_t - 1)
backtestMetrics.summary: Root-mean-square of the percent drawdowns over time; penalizes both the depth and the duration of drawdowns, capturing the 'how stressful was the ride' that MaxDD alone misses.

backtestMetrics.name: Win Rate
backtestMetrics.formula: WinRate = (number of winning trades or periods) / (total trades or periods)
backtestMetrics.summary: Fraction of trades (or holding periods) that were profitable. Interpret alongside profit factor â€” a low win rate can still be highly profitable if winners dwarf losers.

backtestMetrics.name: Profit Factor
backtestMetrics.summary: Total money won divided by total money lost. PF>1 is profitable; >1.5 is solid, >2 strong. Robust summary of the win/loss magnitude trade-off independent of win rate.

backtestMetrics.name: Exposure
backtestMetrics.formula: Exposure = (number of bars with a non-zero position) / (total bars)
backtestMetrics.summary: Fraction of time the strategy is actually invested (in-market). Low exposure with comparable returns means capital is idle and risk-adjusted efficiency per unit of market time is high.

backtestMetrics.name: Turnover
backtestMetrics.summary: Annualized portfolio turnover: half the sum of absolute weight changes per rebalance, annualized. Proxies trading cost drag; high-turnover strategies need a larger gross edge to survive frictions.

backtestMetrics.name: Volatility (Annualized)
backtestMetrics.summary: Annualized standard deviation of daily strategy returns; the denominator of Sharpe and the headline total-risk figure. Compare to the asset/benchmark vol to gauge risk reduction.

backtestMetrics.name: CVaR / Expected Shortfall (95%)
backtestMetrics.summary: Average loss on the worst 5% of days â€” the coherent tail-risk metric. More conservative and better-behaved than VaR for sizing and for comparing strategies' crash behavior.

backtestMetrics.name: Beta to Benchmark
backtestMetrics.summary: Sensitivity of strategy returns to the benchmark (SPY); low/zero beta indicates market-neutral or defensive behavior, useful for separating skill (alpha) from market exposure.

backtestMetrics.name: Information Ratio
backtestMetrics.formula: IR = sqrt(252) * mean(r_strategy - r_benchmark) / std(r_strategy - r_benchmark)
backtestMetrics.summary: Annualized active return (vs SPY) per unit of tracking error; measures consistency of out- or under-performance against the benchmark, the active-management analogue of Sharpe.
